coredata.dc:title: Microwave enhanced carbon dots synthesis from eggshell membrane: Versatile applications in heavy metal ion sensing, strain free detection of fingerprints, UV shielding, food packing and anti-counterfeiting

coredata.dc:description: In this study, a one-step microwave irradiation process combined with a sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution allowed for the quick and easy synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from eggshell membrane (ESM) ashes. The resultant CDs demonstrated exceptional selectivity for Fe3+ and excellent fluorescence (FL) with a quantum yield (QY) of 15.3 %. Utilizing synthetic CDs, a sensitive nanoprobe is utilized to identify free Fe3+ within the 0–350 μM (R2 = 0.9976) range, with a 0.281 μM limit of detection (LoD). Additionally, the CDs were shown to be useful for intracellular Fe3+ detection applications because of their low cytotoxicity and biocompatibility. Investigations using real samples at 475 nm excitation wavelength showed promising results in terms of real-world application. In order to transform molecular data into FL signal outputs, a multi-input logic gate is also constructed. In the UV-A (93 %), UV-B (88 %), UV-C (98 %) and high energy blue light (HEBL) (79 %) regions, blue colour-emitting CDs had the maximum UV blockage, but pure polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) absorbed less than 22–30 % of the light in all UV regions. The integration of CDs into the polymer improved the thermal characteristics of the PVA film. Additionally, testing of in vitro cell viability demonstrated that the CDs, when embedded in the PVA, did not cause cytotoxicity to the Neuroblastoma cell line (SH-SY-5Y). As a paradigm for perishable commodities, the effects of CDs and PVA were assessed on the shelf life of tomatoes. When fresh tomatoes were coated with CDs@PVA and PVA, weight and moisture loss were effectively decreased, according to the results of the long-term monitoring study. Throughout almost 36 days at room temperature, it also dramatically reduced the growth of fungi and prevented spoiling, all the while maintaining the fruits original colour and appearance. The CDs FL under 365 nm UV light excitation makes them well-suited for creating fluorescent inks to deter counterfeiting. Additionally, the study explored the CDs exceptional FL properties for use as a luminescent fingerprint powder, aiding in the detection of latent fingerprints (LFPs) on various surfaces. The findings from this study highlight the potential of cost-effective and safe CDs for a range of applications, including non-cytotoxic UV blocking, active packaging, Fe3+ sensing, fingerprint detection, anti-counterfeiting (AC) measures and flexible nanocomposite (NC) films.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a one-step microwave irradiation process combined with a sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution allowed for the quick and easy synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from eggshell membrane (ESM) ashes. The resultant CDs demonstrated exceptional selectivity for Fe3+ and excellent fluorescence (FL) with a quantum yield (QY) of 15.3 %. Utilizing synthetic CDs, a sensitive nanoprobe is utilized to identify free Fe3+ within the 0–350 μM (R2 = 0.9976) range, with a 0.281 μM limit of detection (LoD). Additionally, the CDs were shown to be useful for intracellular Fe3+ detection applications because of their low cytotoxicity and biocompatibility. Investigations using real samples at 475 nm excitation wavelength showed promising results in terms of real-world application. In order to transform molecular data into FL signal outputs, a multi-input logic gate is also constructed. In the UV-A (93 %), UV-B (88 %), UV-C (98 %) and high energy blue light (HEBL) (79 %) regions, blue colour-emitting CDs had the maximum UV blockage, but pure polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) absorbed less than 22–30 % of the light in all UV regions. The integration of CDs into the polymer improved the thermal characteristics of the PVA film. Additionally, testing of in vitro cell viability demonstrated that the CDs, when embedded in the PVA, did not cause cytotoxicity to the Neuroblastoma cell line (SH-SY-5Y). As a paradigm for perishable commodities, the effects of CDs and PVA were assessed on the shelf life of tomatoes. When fresh tomatoes were coated with CDs@PVA and PVA, weight and moisture loss were effectively decreased, according to the results of the long-term monitoring study. Throughout almost 36 days at room temperature, it also dramatically reduced the growth of fungi and prevented spoiling, all the while maintaining the fruits original colour and appearance. The CDs FL under 365 nm UV light excitation makes them well-suited for creating fluorescent inks to deter counterfeiting. Additionally, the study explored the CDs exceptional FL properties for use as a luminescent fingerprint powder, aiding in the detection of latent fingerprints (LFPs) on various surfaces. The findings from this study highlight the potential of cost-effective and safe CDs for a range of applications, including non-cytotoxic UV blocking, active packaging, Fe3+ sensing, fingerprint detection, anti-counterfeiting (AC) measures and flexible nanocomposite (NC) films.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots, sometimes referred to as CDs or CQDs, are a new type of carbon material that have particles less than 10 nm. They were initially found in 2004 while employing electrophoresis to purify one-dimensional carbon nanotubes [1]. CDs have garnered a lot of attention since afterwards. Carbon quantum dots have been created using a variety of synthetic techniques, including bottom-up techniques such as pyrolysis, chemical oxidation, microwave treatment and hydrothermal synthesis, as well as top-down techniques like arc discharge, laser ablation and plasma-induced fabrication [2]. CDs provide a number of benefits, such as great solubility in a variety of solvents, strong analytical selectivity and sensitivity, photostability and non-toxicity [3]. Consequently, recent research has explored their applications across diverse fields, such as chemical sensing, photocatalysis, solar cells, bio-imaging and biosensing [4,5]. Numerous experiments on synthesizing CDs have utilized carbon-rich resources, pine tannin, including egg whites, orange juice, lignin, banana juice and ESM [6–10]. ESM is becoming more and more well-known for its use as a bio-template for the creation of nanoparticles, a sorbent for organics and heavy metals, an essential ingredient of biosensors and a substance with a range of medical uses. It is made up of a fibrous structure that is situated in between the egg white and eggshell [11]. Because ESM is widely available and reasonably priced, it is selected as the carbon source. Our approach has a few advantages over earlier research [12]: it uses waste materials from homes, only needs a 200 °C pre-treatment temperature and synthesises in ∼1 min.

All living things require iron, which is present in large amounts in the surrounding environment. In physiological activities including cellular metabolism, oxygen transport and enzymatic reactions, iron is essential [13]. Insufficient amounts of iron, however, can lead to a number of health issues, including as heart disease, cancer and organ failure. For metal ion detection, a number of analytical techniques are used, such as voltammetry, spectrophotometry and FL sensing. Of these, FL sensing stands out for its convenience, rapid response, selectivity and high sensitivity, making it particularly effective for detecting trace metal ion concentrations. In recent years, there has been growing research into using CDs for Fe3+ detection. Finding a way to achieve high QY and selectivity while using CDs as fluorescent probes is the major obstacle. It is known that improve ion selectivity and QY of CDs can be achieved by surface modification and doping. Among the many benefits when employing biomass as a carbon source for CDs is its remarkable QY, which allows for self-doping, especially N-doping, without requiring further chemicals or conversions. Table 1 shows the literature review of CDs derived from various sources with diverse applications [14–29].

FP analysis is a robust and dependable method for individual identification, employed in forensic investigations for over a century. Among various fingerprint imaging techniques, powder dusting is particularly popular due to its simplicity, ease of development and low cost. Numerous luminous materials, like semiconductor quantum dots and heavy metal dyes, can cause environmental harm or FL dampening when they aggregate in the solid state. This severely limits its application in the imaging and detection of LFPs. Thus, there is a need to develop a fluorescent material that is environmentally friendly and exhibits strong FL in the solid state for LFPs imaging. CDs, as inexpensive and low-toxicity nanomaterials with excellent FL properties, are promising candidates for visualizing LFPs [30–32].

It is now impossible to avoid being around electronic devices including light-emitting diodes (LEDs), cell phones, tablets and compact fluorescent lamps (CFLs) [33]. Consequently, developing UV-blocking materials by incorporating UV absorbers is increasingly valuable for practical applications. Although hazardous light is commonly categorized as UV-C (100–280 nm), UV-B (280–315 nm), UV-A (315–400 nm) and HEBL(400–450 nm), its spectral range typically spans from 100 to 450 nm [34]. The majority of UV and HEBL emitted by electronics and sunshine is damaging to fruits, polymers and eyes. Graphene nanoparticles and inorganic particles like TiO2, SiO2 and ZnO are examples of traditional UV absorbers [35]. While most traditional UV absorbers exhibit effective UV-blocking properties, in addition to having poor flame resistance, many metal-based nanoparticles may also have catalytic properties that allow them to break down polymeric matrix [36]. Because of its exceptional film-forming capabilities, biodegradability, high transparency, PVA is a desirable coating material. Flexible fibers, adhesives, wearable technology, shielding and packaging materials are among the many applications [37]. Nevertheless, pure PVA is quickly ignited and brittle because to its low mechanical and flame retardant qualities. PVA also has a tendency to burn quickly and form large melt droplets, both of which can accelerate the spread of flames. The practical use of PVA films in applications demanding high strength and fire resistance has been hampered by these shortcomings. Previous publications [38] have shown that the efficient reinforcing action of nanocellulose in NCs allows PVA films containing relatively modest amounts of nanocellulose (∼1.0 wt%) to preserve outstanding transparency and robust mechanical characteristics. Consequently, it is important to produce UV-blocking films with a natural PVA/nanocellulose matrix to filter damaging light without sacrificing high strength, visibility, or flame retardant qualities.

Reducing food waste is crucial for building a sustainable economy. Every year, between one-third and half of the food produced worldwide is lost or wasted as a result of spoiling from microorganisms or shelf-life expiration. According to a 2020 report by the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO), 14 % of food meant for human consumption is wasted before it could be sold [39]. Microbiological contamination is a major danger factor for perishable crops, like fresh fruits and vegetables with high metabolic activity. This may cause quick spoiling, resulting in changes in taste, colour and odor. Food packaging is essential for maintaining food safety and quality because it protects food from chemicals, environmental viruses and microbes, extending its shelf life and preventing spoiling.

Polymer NCs that are recyclable, offer vital features like oxygen barriers, antibacterial and antifungal qualities and the appropriate color and look can be created in order to progress food packaging technology. In order to do this, natural polymers used in food packaging, such as starch, chitosan, alginate and cellulose and synthetic polymers, such as polyethylene, polypropylene, PVA and polyvinyl chloride (PVC), are combined with nanoscale fillers [40]. Furthermore, because materials in contact with food are often exposed to ultraviolet light from the sun, UV-absorbing fillers are added to a variety of polymeric materials, including as collagen, polyimides, PVA and cellulose to improve UV shielding qualities and stop degradation. The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has approved PVA, a synthetic polymer that is edible, biodegradable, non-toxic and ecologically friendly [41]. It can be used in human therapeutic uses. Because of PVA superior oxygen barrier qualities, powerful mechanical attributes and efficient film-forming ability, it has been used as an ingredient in food product coatings and packaging [42]. Nevertheless, PVA presents challenges including inadequate thermal stability and biological activity, as well as significant moisture absorption. Moreover, it provides only mediocre protection in food packaging systems against UV rays. PVA now contains organic, inorganic and carbon dot nanoparticles to enhance these properties and add additional usefulness to the polymer [43]. By utilizing the hydrogen bonding groups found in the PVA structure, this method makes it possible to combine additional nanomaterials to create functional NCs.

The food and pharmaceutical industries can involve fraudulent tactics that seriously jeopardize public health. Over the past few decades, a variety of security technologies, holograms, including as security inks, simple markers and plasma security labels have been created to combat counterfeiting [44]. CDs have attracted attention as AC inks because of their many benefits, which include good FL stability, affordability, ease of production and environmental friendliness. Mopan persimmons were utilized as a carbon source to create CDs, which were then combined with sugar to effectively prevent counterfeiting. As such, CDs generated from biomass have great potential for use in AC applications [45].

Here, we present a straightforward method for producing CDs using a one-step microwave approach. The resulting CDs were effectively utilized to detect LFPs rapidly, demonstrating their outstanding imaging capability on various substrate surfaces. To contribute to the detection of Fe3+, we have developed a FL-based sensor using carbon quantum dots. This sensor offers affordability, non-toxicity and scalability for large-scale production [46]. Additionally, it exhibits excellent sensitivity and selectivity, functioning effectively even under high ionic concentrations. Furthermore, CDs were employed as AC ink, with information clearly visible on different surfaces under UV light. To extend the shelf life of perishable foods like tomatoes, edible and UV-protective coatings made of CDs embedded in a PVA polymer matrix were employed. The thermal and optical characteristics of NCs films were thoroughly examined.

The ESM is delicately removed from a freshly obtained eggshell sourced from the student mess of Tumkur University. PVA, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), Quinine sulfate (QS), H2O2, AgNO3, Al2(SO4)3·18 H2O, CuCl2·2 H2O, FeCl2·4 H2O, FeCl3·6 H2O, KCl, MgSO4, NiCl2, Pb(NO3)2, MnCl₂, CdCl₂ and Zn(Oac)2·2 H2O were procured from SRL, India and used as received without additional purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of egg shell membrane derived carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The process of creating CDs involved heating ashes made from ESMs in a microwave (700 W) to facilitate the process. Initially, the ESM is meticulously separated from a fresh eggshell and subsequently rinsed multiple times with deionized water to eliminate excess albumen and impurities. For the production of ESM ashes, the purified ESMs is dried and burned for 3 h at 450 °C in a muffle furnace. In a standard synthesis procedure, 3 mg of these ashes were combined with 5 mL of NaOH solution (1 mol L−1). After that, the mixture is subjected to heat for 5 min in a home microwave oven. NaOH was used to adjust the pH of the carbon dot solution to enhance the fluorescence properties and stability of the CDs. To remove unreacted ashes, the resultant slurry is centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min after being diluted with deionized water. The excess NaOH is then eliminated from the solution by dialysis against deionized water for two days. The solution is then stored at 4 °C for further analysis and uses. Fig. 1a shows a schematic depiction of the procedure used to create CDs.

Fe³⁺ ions were identified using fluorescence spectra in aqueous solutions. In brief, CDs were dissolved in sodium acetate buffer (Na-Ac) at pH approximately 7, with a concentration of 20 μg/mL. FeCl3⋅6 H2O was then added at various concentrations (0, 10, 20, 40, 80, 160, and 320 μM). Following a 10-minute incubation at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity was recorded at 475 nm with an excitation wavelength of 375 nm. The selectivity for iron was evaluated by adding other metal ions (Zn²⁺, Ag⁺, Pb²⁺, Mg²⁺, Fe²⁺, Cu²⁺, K⁺, Ni²⁺, Mn²⁺, Cd²⁺, and Al³⁺) at a concentration of 200 μM, employing the same methods.

Following procedures described in the literature, the FL QY of CDs is calculated using QS as the standard and 0.1 M H2SO4 as the solvent (Φst = 0.54 at 360 nm). The formula given below is used to determine the QY.

(1), the FL QY is represented by the letter " Ф " the integral area of the emission intensity in the FL spectrum is indicated by the letter "I" and the samples refractive index is found in Eq. (1). QS is indicated by the subscript 'st'. All analyzed samples had their absorbance intensity kept below 0.1 in order to reduce the effects of reabsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognizing along with visualizing latent fingerprints
ce:section.ce:para: Every relevant fingerprint used in this study came from a single adult male participant who is 25 years old. The fingerprint suppliers had to wipe their fingers with hand sanitizer before softly brushing their foreheads with them and rubbing them against different surfaces like metal, glass and Al. Foil. The specific steps for LFP detection were as follows: A feather brush is used to apply the powder onto the fingerprint by dipping it onto the CDs, then shaking the powder over the fingerprint. The excess floating powder is carefully brushed away, leaving the powder distributed on the fingerprint. Under UV irradiation, the FPs emitted a bright blue FL. Photographs of all the FPs were captured using a Canon 850D camera.

After adding 10 g of PVA and 4 g of glycerol to 200 mL of distilled water, the mixture is magnetically agitated for 3 h at 90 °C. Subsequently, the solution is mixed with CDs (at 1, 3 and 5 wt% based on PVA) at room temperature (25 ± 2 °C) for 15 min at a speed of 200 rpm. After that, the resultant film solution is degassed by stirring for 2 h at 80 rpm. After preparing the film solution, it is poured onto a glass plate coated with Teflon and let to dry for 72 h at room temperature. Prior to assessing their characteristics, the films were dried, then scraped off the plate and conditioned for 24 h at 25 °C and 50 % relative humidity (RH). The resulting NC films had labels that said neat PVA, 1 wt% CDs @PVA, 3 wt% CDs @PVA and 5 wt% CDs @PVA, based on concentration of CDs were added. The construction method for the NCs film is schematically represented in Fig. 1 b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water contact angle (WCA) and mechanical properties
ce:section.ce:para: WCA of PVA and 1–5 wt% CDs@PVA NCs films were recorded as described in previous literature [47] and the contact angles were measured using ImageJ software [48]. The CDs@PVA NCs films were secured on a smooth metal base and deionized water (4 μL), juice, milk and castor oil were gradually dropped onto the film surfaces. After 1 min, the WCA of every sample is determined. A universal tensile testing apparatus (Instron 3343, USA) is used for the mechanical testing, with a crosshead speed of 50 mm/min. The NCs films of CDs@PVA were sliced into standard samples with dimensions of 25 mm×5 mm. The average of the three measurements is given as the final result.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coating of tomatoes with PVA, CDs and 5 wt% CDs @PVA NCs
ce:section.ce:para: From the grocery store, fresh tomatoes were bought. These tomatoes were carefully selected to ensure uniformity in size, shape, colour, texture and to be free of blemishes, visible defects and decay before applying any coatings. The tomatoes were first cleaned and dried. After cleaning, the tomatoes were twice dip-coated in PVA, CDs and 5 wt% CDs @PVA NCs. Following coating, the tomatoes both coated and uncoated were sent to dry at room temperature (25 °C) by hanging them on a drying device. Images of the tomatoes were captured using a digital camera at intervals from 0 to 36 days for visual assessment. At least four tomatoes were used for each repetition.

SH-SY-5Y neuroblastoma cells, which were acquired from the National Center for Cell Science (NCCS) in Pune, were used in the cytotoxicity assay. The impact of CDs on these cells is assessed using the MTT (3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) test. To summarize, a 96-well plate is seeded with cells and they were cultured at 37°C in 5 % CO2 at a concentration of 1 × 106 cells/mL. Following a 24 h period, 10 µL of MTT reagent and diverse CD concentrations (10–50 µL in 1 mL of PBS) were incorporated into the cell growth media. After that, the cells were incubated for an hour at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 in the air. At 450 nm, the optical density (OD) is determined with a microplate reader. Furthermore, the cells were inspected at a 20× magnification with a fluorescent microscope. This formula is used to determine cell viability [49]:
(2)

To prepare the AC ink, dissolve 0.7 g of PVA in 10 mL of water and then add 2 mL of CDs solution to the mixture. Sonicate the blend for 30 min. The resulting solution can be used as AC fluorescent ink for writing and printing on various substrates using a simple brush or screen-printing technique. The handwritten and printed AC designs were photographed under daylight and UV 365 nm light, respectively. The developed AC patterns under UV 365 nm are displayed in Fig. 1 b.

To determine the crystalline phases and amorphous content in the samples, X-ray diffraction (XRD) is used. A powder X-ray diffractometer Shimadzu 7000 is used, with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å). A scanning range of 10–60 °, a step size of 0.02° and a scan rate of 2.4 °/min were used for the measurements. A modified AlKα source and an XR4 dual anode were used in a VG ESCALAB MKII spectrometer to perform X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments. Using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS-10 spectrometer (USA), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were captured spanning a wavelength range of 450 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. A confocal Raman spectrometer (model 500, STR 500) with an excitation laser wavelength of 532 nm is used to analyze the morphology and functional groups of the materials. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging is used with a JEOL JEM 2100 equipment running at 200 kV to ascertain the morphology and particle size of the CDs. Using ImageJ software, more than 30 particles were analyzed in order to determine the size distribution of CDs. A Libra S12 UV–vis spectrophotometer (UK) is used to record the room temperature absorption spectra of the CDs, spanning a wavelength range of 200–700 nm at 5-nm intervals. Using a Horiba spectrofluorimeter and a 450 W Xenon lamp, photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained. Using a microcomputer-controlled electronic universal testing equipment (CMT6503), the mechanical characteristics of the CDs composite films were assessed. A tensile rate of 50 mm/min is applied to the sample, which had dimensions of 50 mm × 5 mm × 0.10 mm. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) were used to examine the thermal characteristics of CDs composite films. Using a JEOL-JEM-2100 PLUS from Japan, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) pictures were taken in order to analyze the CDs morphology and determine whether or not the PVA matrix and CDs were compatible. Utilizing Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS), the elemental distribution of the leftover char is examined.

TEM is used to analyze the shape, size and surface morphology of CDs ( Fig. 2a). The results of the research show that the CDs have particle sizes ranging from 2 to 7 nm and that they are not evenly dispersed. Furthermore, lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.22 nm are clearly visible in high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) pictures of all samples (Fig. 2a). These fringes correlate to the (100) lattice distance of graphitic carbon. Using ImageJ software, the particle size distribution is generated by randomly counting 30 particles from the TEM images (Fig. 2b). This resulted in an average particle size of 4.3 ± 0.6 nm. Fig. 2c displays the SEM image that is used to study the surface morphology of the CDs. The SEM pictures show distinct-edged, spherically-shaped particles, which validate the TEM findings. The EDS analysis identified the presence of nitrogen, oxygen and carbon, as seen in the spectrum in Figure S1a. These elements are equally distributed, as seen by the elemental mapping micrographs (Figure S1b-d). This confirms the efficiency of the microwave technique and is in line with the elemental composition shown by the EDS results.

Fig. 2d depicts the diffraction pattern of the formed carbon CDs, which shows peaks at 2θ = 19.78° and 40.96°, respectively, which corresponds to the (002) and (100) planes of carbonaceous substances [50]. As a consequence of interlayer packing, a turbostratic structure is formed in the (002) plane due to the arrangement of disordered carbon atoms within the hexagonal graphite structure of the CDs (JCPDS No. 26–1076) [51]. Consequently, the main characteristic of the CDs is their graphitic carbon framework, which has interlayer spacings of 0.38 nm and 0.21 nm, respectively, corresponding to the (002) and (100) diffraction planes [52]. The amorphous character of the carbon structure is responsible for the observed spacing being larger than the d-spacing of the (002) planes in typical bulk graphite, which is ∼ 0.34 nm. Since carbon nanostructures are predominantly made up of closely spaced carbon and oxygen atoms, these two diffraction peaks are indicative of carbon nanostructures [53].

The level of graphitization in the carbon structure is also assessed using Raman spectroscopy. The Raman spectra showing the D and G bands are shown in Fig. 2e. The D band, which is generally seen at 1369 cm−1, is associated with imperfections and disarray in the carbon structure [54]. The sp2 hybridized C-C bonds in the CDs are represented by the G band, which is normally detected at approximately 1584 cm−1 [55]. The moderate degree of graphitization and partially disordered structure of the CDs are indicated by the D to G band intensity ratio of 0.86. The outcomes align with the XRD observations. XPS elemental analysis is used to examine the prepared CDs chemical composition and various forms of bonds. Three peaks at 284.2, 400.5 and 530.7 eV, which correspond to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively, can be seen in the CDs XPS survey scan spectrum, which is displayed in Fig. 2f. Fig. 2g-i show the produced CDs deconvoluted XPS spectra for the C1s, N1s and O1s. Three peaks, correspondingly situated at 284.2, 285.6 and 287.7 eV in the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. 2g), are ascribed to CC, C–N and CO bonds. Fig. 2h displays the high-resolution N 1 s spectra, which feature three peaks with binding energy values of 398.7, 400.5 and 402.4 eV, correspond to N–H, C–N, and CN nitrogen types, respectively. Two peaks can be seen in the O 1 s XPS spectra (Fig. 2i) at 532.5 and 530.7 eV, respectively. These correspond to C–O and CO bonds.

Figure S2a-d displays the SEM photographs of the PVA and 1–5 wt% CDs@PVA NCs films. Because of their uniformity and flatness, NC films are easier to produce and use in solid-state FL devices (Figure S2b-d). When the amount of CDs in the NCs film increases, the color turns brown, but the microscopic appearance of the films stays mostly unchanged. This is because the CDs are too small to be directly detected, with an average diameter of typically less than 10 nm [56]. But in the 5 wt% CDs@PVA composite film, flaws and wrinkles start to show up with a higher content of CDs (Figure S2d). The aggregation and separation of the CDs is probably the cause of this. Next, powder XRD patterns were used to examine the phase structures of CDs, PVA and 1–5 wt% CDs@PVA NC films. The semi-crystalline structure of the clean PVA film is responsible for the peaks at 2θ values of 19° and 40°. Strong hydrogen connections between the molecular chains of the PVA matrix produce crystalline areas, which are shown by these prominent peaks (Figure S2e). The graphite (002) plane is represented by a single broad peak at 2θ = 19.45° in the CDs XRD pattern (Figure S2e). In contrast, the primary diffraction peak can be found at 2θ = 19.64° in the XRD pattern of the pure PVA film. The PVA diffraction peak position stays constant while 1–5 % CDs are doped, but the diffraction peak strength at 19.45° steadily rises, signifying a greater doping quantity of CDs. The interaction between the hydroxyl groups in PVA and the various functional groups (NH2) in the CDs is responsible for this improvement. The crystalline nature of the composite membrane decreases as a result of PVA molecules finding it more difficult to pass through the lattice due to the action of these micro-cross-linking and other processes. The outcomes provide more evidence that the CDs have been successfully incorporated into the PVA film.

To evaluate the effect of CDs on the level of graphitization in the remaining char, Raman spectroscopy is utilized. Two Raman absorption peaks at 1350 and 1590 cm−1, corresponding to disordered carbon (D) and graphitic carbon (G), respectively [57], are visible in the residual char of all samples, as shown in Figure S2f. The integrated peak area ratio (ID/IG) between the D peak and the G peak is typically used to determine the residual chars degree of graphitization. A higher level of graphitization is indicated by smaller ID/IG values. The ID/IG value in the pure PVA sample is 3.48 and when 1 wt% CDs were added, the value marginally dropped to 3.39. Further reductions in ID/IG values to 3.20 and 3.02 were seen upon the addition of 3 and 5 wt% CDs to PVA [58]. This suggests that there is a significant improvement in the residual chars degree of graphitization, indicating a better barrier effect during the preparation procedure.

PL and UV–vis absorption spectra were used to investigate the optical characteristics of the CDs in an aqueous solution, as shown in Fig. 3a. The aqueous CDs solution UV–vis absorption spectrum, as depicted in the image, showed a modest peak at 265 nm, which is associated with the CC bonds π–π* electronic transition [59]. Moreover, when stimulated at 375 nm, the CDs emission spectra revealed a maximum emission at 475 nm (Fig. 3a). Furthermore, the CIE diagram for the CDs is shown in Fig. 3b. The coordinates (0.118, 0.241) provided by the Commission Internationale de l′Éclairage (CIE) representation are extremely close to the blue region [60]. This is in agreement with the CD photo and the emission wavelength. Furthermore, QS and CD solutions UV–Vis absorption and PL emission spectra were utilized to estimate the QY (Fig. 3c and d). It is determined that the CDs have an estimated QY of 15.3 %.

The measurement of CDs excitation-dependent FL emission spectra involved adjusting the excitation wavelength between 335 and 380 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 3 e. Utilizing a 375 nm excitation wavelength, the peak emission intensity is measured at 475 nm [61]. Interestingly, the emission peak position did not change when the excitation wavelength changed, indicating that CDs exhibit excitation-independent FL activity. With respect to a QS standard, the CDs FL QY is found to be 15.3 %, demonstrating exceptional FL efficiency. Fig. 3 f displays the PL spectra of the CDs. A clear emission is seen from the CDs, as the picture illustrates, with the emission peak migrating from around 481 nm to 490 nm when the excitation wavelength switches from 335 to 380 nm nm. The emission response of CDs is typically dependent on stimulation [62].

Investigations were conducted on the effects of pH on FL performance at different pH levels (3, 4, 6, 8, 10 and 12). Figure S3a examines and shows the PL spectra of CDs at these various pH values. As shown in the figure, the maximum emission wavelength remains nearly constant across different pH conditions. The peak emission intensity is observed at pH ∼4, while the intensity shows minimal variation at pH 6, 8, 10 and 12. This suggests that the CDs show strong stability throughout a wide pH range, ranging from 3 to 12. Acidic and amine groups on the surface of CDs may be protonated and deprotonated, which would explain the drop in FL intensity at both low and high pH values. Figure S3b displays the normalized emission intensities of the CDs, which show a gradual increase in intensity up to pH ∼4. Subsequently, the intensity begins to decrease as the pH increases from 6 to 12. The corresponding photographic images of the CDs treated at different pH levels are shown in Figure S3c.

To evaluate the stability of photobleaching, the CDs solution is subjected to UV light (8 W) at 365 nm for a duration of 10–80 min. The intensity of FL is then evaluated following each UV treatment. The variations in CDs FL intensity at different irradiation times (0–80 min) are shown in Figure S3d. Even after 80 min of intense UV light exposure, the CDs retain their initial FL intensity. Moreover, the normalized spectra in Figure S3e demonstrate that the synthesized CDs exhibit minimal or insignificant changes in FL stability after UV irradiation. The digital pictures of the UV-treated CDs at different times are shown in Figure S3f. The CDs emission spectra showed no discernible changes even after they were exposed to UV light (365 nm) continuously for up to 80 min, as depicted in the figures. This suggests that the CDs solution has good photostability.

Longevity of the developed CDs over time is important for real-time optical applications. Once extended storage is implemented, the FL intensity of the CDs aqueous solution is measured at different storage intervals (1–60 days) (Figure S3g). There is no discernible aggregation or change in FL intensity even after an extended period of storage. This stability is probably brought about by the CDs surface functionalization, which contributes charges or polar functional groups that strengthen electrostatic repulsions and support stability between the CDs. Figure S3h displays the normalized emission intensities of the CDs, revealing only a slight decrease in intensity as storage time increases. Figure S3i shows digital images of the CDs at various storage times. These observations suggest that the CDs maintain stable FL over long periods.

Despite being widely employed for Fe3+ detection, CDs limited specificity has limited their applicability. According to earlier studies, when CDs are employed to detect Fe3+, copper and zinc ions frequently tamper with the FL intensity [63]. Subsequently, different metal ions such as Zn2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, K+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cd2+ and Al3+ have been included to test the selectivity of CDs for Fe3+ [64]. The FL emission intensity ratios, denoted as F/F0 (where F is the FL intensity when metal ions are present and F0 is the intensity when they are absent), were assessed at a 200 μM concentration for every metal ion ( Fig. 4a). When Fe3+ is added, as Fig. 4b illustrates, the FL intensity dramatically dropped, while the effect of other metal ions is negligible. Fig. 4c shows digital photos of the CDs taken under day light and UV 365 nm light. Significantly more FL is quenched by Fe3+ than by Fe2+ demonstrating that Fe3+ increased charge density leads to a stronger attraction for the trivalent metal ions. Nevertheless, CDs exhibit higher selectivity for Fe3+ since Al3+ coordination capacity is weaker than Fe3+. The reason for this Fe3+ selectivity is that the CDs facilitate the creation of surface complexes by donating electron pairs to the unoccupied d orbitals of Fe3+ in its excited state through a non-radiative electron transfer process. It is possible to speculate that CDs made from ESM have a significant binding to Fe3+ due to their abundance of functional groups, such as hydroxyl groups [65]. The FL spectra were examined as a function of concentration in order to investigate the quenching impact of Fe3+ in more detail. Fig. 4d shows that when the concentration of Fe3+ rises, the CDs solution FL intensity at 475 nm gradually reduced. Furthermore, a significant linear relationship with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9976 is seen in Fig. 4e between the FL intensity ratios (F0/F) and Fe3+ concentration within the linear range of 0–350 μM. With a S/N ratio of 3, the LoD is estimated to be 0.281 μM, which is less than that of CDs synthesized from different biomass precursors ( Table 2) [66–75]. This suggests that CDs hold considerable promise as sensors for detecting Fe³⁺ ions. To assess the detection of Fe³⁺, the optical properties of the CDs synthesized from waste raw materials were compared with those reported in previous studies (Table 2). Figure S4 presents a schematic illustration of the FL quenching method. The images of the cuvette clearly illustrate the addition of Fe³⁺ to the CDs solution results in a significant decrease in intensity.

In order to assess the coordination contacts between CDs and Fe3+ ions even more, the CDs-Fe3+ complex is treated with an EDTA solution in order to see if the intensity of the FL could be recovered. When Fe3+ is present at a 200 μM concentration, as Fig. 4f illustrates, the FL intensity of CDs dramatically dropped. On the other hand, the CDs-Fe3+ complexes FL intensity raise by more than two times after the EDTA solution is added (Fig. 4g). If cyclodextrins are present, they can also interact with Fe³⁺ ions, potentially displacing them from the EDTA complex. This competition can result in reversible FL recovery. In other words, if cyclodextrins remove Fe³⁺ ions from the EDTA complex, the FL of the system may change, indicating the presence of free or differently coordinated Fe³⁺ ions. The process is "reversible" because the complexation and displacement reactions can go back and forth depending on the relative concentrations and affinities of the complexing agents (EDTA and CDs) for Fe³⁺ ions. This reversibility is key for using such systems in applications like sensing or controlled release. Furthermore, compared to the interactions between EDTA and CDs-Fe3+ complexes, the change in FL intensity caused by EDTA and CDs contacts is substantially smaller. The findings suggest that when it comes to complexing with Fe3+, CDs are not as effective as the chelating agent EDTA.

The design of a molecular logic gate is influenced by different substances, including EDTA and Fe3+ on the FL spectra of CDs. We used a simple chemical process called the Fenton reaction, which converts Fe2+ to Fe3+ in the presence of H2O2, to create a multiple-input, single-output logic gate. This reaction is represented by the following equation [76]:
(3) e2+ + H2O2/Fe3+ + OH

Utilizing this understanding, we examined the impact of Fe2+ by itself, as well as the combination of Fe2+ + H2O2 and Fe2+ + H2O2 + EDTA, on the CDs emission signal [77]. In the initial stages of the experiment, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was indeed reduced upon the addition of Fe²⁺ and H₂O₂, which is indicative of quenching due to the interaction between the CDs and these species ( Fig. 5a). However, when EDTA was subsequently added, it chelated the Fe²⁺ ions, effectively removing them from the solution. This chelation interrupts the quenching effect and allows for a partial recovery of the fluorescence intensity. It is important to note that while we observed an increase in fluorescence intensity upon the addition of EDTA, the intensity may not fully recover to the levels observed with the standalone CDs or the CDs+Fe²⁺ solution without H₂O₂. This is likely due to several factors, including residual effects of H₂O₂ on the CDs and the potential formation of different reactive species during the reaction. Thus, the restoration of fluorescence intensity is relative and may not equate to a complete recovery to baseline levels. The input signal is determined by the analytes condition, where its presence is denoted by 1 and its absence by 0. The output signal represents the FL state: 1 denotes normal FL intensity and 0 denotes quenched FL. Fe3+ is the only input used in the design of a single-input NOT gate and the output is the CDs FL intensity (Fig. 5b). Upon detection of Fe3+ in the CDs system (input "1"), the logical value "1" is changed to "0." On the other hand, when Fe3+ is absent (input="0"), the result is "1". Given that EDTA can be added to the CDs-Fe3+ system to restore its FL, a two-input logic gate is developed using EDTA as input 2 and Fe3+ as input 1 (Fig. 5c). The output is low (output "0") when the only element present is Fe3+ (input "1 0"). Output "1" indicates that the FL output is still high for all other input combinations. Fe2+ and H2O2 were used in the design of another molecular logic gate to provide information about the oxidation process of Fe2+ to Fe3+. The CDs FL doesn't change when Fe2+ or H2O2 are the only elements present. But in the presence of both Fe2+ and H2O2, the FL dramatically quenches because Fe3+ is formed. Fig. 5d shows how to design a NAND gate based on the outputs. Further, Fe3+ is the first input, Fe2+ is the second and H2O2 is the third input in a triple-input logic gate design. Fig. 5e displays the related truth table, which shows that the FL probe (CDs) responds less to Fe2+ and more strongly to Fe3+. This configuration makes a clear distinction between Fe3+ and Fe2+. Finally, Fe3+, EDTA, Fe2+ and H2O2 are configured as the first, second, third and fourth inputs, respectively, of a four-input logic gate. The truth table shown in Fig. 5f summarizes the corresponding FL results. The development of FL-based smart molecular devices can benefit from the use of multi-input logic gates [78].

The sebaceous and eccrine glands are the two main glands from which the LFPs are secreted. Only the palms of the hands and the soles of the feet have eccrine glands. Eccrine gland secretions are mostly composed of water, protein molecules, amino acids, with trace amounts of organic substances like sugars and inorganic salts like sulfates, phosphates and chlorides [79,80]. Sebaceous glands, on the other hand, are connected to hair roots and are located all over the body with the exception of the palms of the hands and the soles of the feet. Sebum is secreted by sebaceous glands and contains fatty acids, squalene and wax esters. As a result of possible interaction with the face or hair, eccrine sweat and sebum make up the majority of fingerprint residue. Furthermore, there may be contamination like makeup or other outside materials.

In real-world applications, a variety of common objects are utilized as substrates for the detection of LFPs. On these various surfaces, the finely defined ridges of LFPs may be successfully identified by the optimized 5 wt% CDs ( Fig. 6a). The residual ridges of LFPs are precisely where the optimized CDs stick, which increases the powder-dusting methods potential effectiveness [81]. The CDs show highly contrasted detailed ridges of LFPs on glass, Al. Foil and metal surfaces, suggesting that these substrates do not significantly introduce background interference into the fluorescent pictures of LFPs. Because there is little overlap between the background FL and the blue FL of the CDs, levels I–III on these substrate surfaces are easily distinguished. This addresses one of the main benefits of employing CDs namely, the reduction of background interference and improvement of the LFPs image quality. These findings show that even in the presence of background FL from the substrates, the blue-emitting CDs provide enough contrast between the background and the blue ridges to enable the clear identification of fine-grained LFP ridge features. The findings suggest that the CDs can effectively display the fine details of LFPs on different substrate surfaces. For FPs developed on each of these surfaces, 3D interactive profiles are created in order to assess the development effect even more (Fig. 6b). A pixel profile analysis is carried out on a number of papillary ridges in order to evaluate the LFPs development effect and sensitivity, as shown by the solid white rectangle in Fig. 6a. Cross-sectional red intensity levels for many papillary ridges are compared in Fig. 6c. With the background effect almost unnoticeable, the red value easily separates the dark-field photos of furrows with low values from the bright-field images of ridges with high values in each fingerprint. As a result, the optimized CDs exhibit encouraging potential in detecting LFP [82–84].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of level I-III features from semi-porous surface
ce:section.ce:para: The process of creating LFPs entails putting the informative characteristics of papillary ridge patterns on display. Usually, there are three levels to this information. Details of ridge patterns, including whorls, arches, delta and loops are covered in Level I. Specific details such bifurcations, short ridges, deltas, lakes, ridge endings, bridges, hooks, enclosures and islands are the subject of Level II. The minutia details and their haphazard arrangements add to a distinctiveness of FPs. Level III also includes the number, ridges position, shape, scars and sweat pores [85–87]. Level III details offer useful reference information for fixing partial flaws or hazy Level II details, even though they might not be enough to detect LFPs on their own. Using the powder dusting approach, the usefulness of CDs powder for the quick viewing of LFPs is examined. Level I details like delta and core (right loop) are readily recognized, as seen in Fig. 7 a. Furthermore, it is easy to see Level II characteristics such as lakes, crossings, islands, dots, enclosures, bridges, hooks and short ridges. Bifurcations and double bifurcations are also easily visible [88]. Furthermore, Level III data is observable, including sweat pores. CDs are a good option for the quick visualization of LFPs in forensic analysis and crime scene investigations since their informative qualities are essential for individual identification. Additionally, the pixel profile of a small region of the fingerprint (Fig. 7 b) shows ridges (in blue) with high pixel values and furrows (in black) with low pixel values. Thus, the pixel profile allows for clear differentiation between the ridges and furrows of a fingerprint. Additionally, it demonstrates that the CDs preferentially adhere to the ridges rather than the furrows. Overall, these details provide conclusive evidence for individual identification and highlight the applicability of our CDs for LFP identification [89–91].

Traditional reagents (fluorescent commercial fingerprint powders in red, green, black and white) were used to compare the performance of 5 wt% CDs in visualizing LFPs. As shown in Fig. 8a, while commercial powders can clearly reveal Level I features, they struggle to distinguish Level II and III details. But because CDs have a smaller particle size and better adherence, they may easily capture Level II and III details like sweat pores from FPs [92]. As a result, the optimized CDs perform better in LFP applications than conventional chemicals, indicating their potential as an ideal material for this use. Fig. 8b displays the 3D profiles of developed FPs on a semi-porous substrate (Al. foil) using ImageJ software. The profiles clearly show that the CDs adhere only to the ridges and not to the furrows. To further evaluate the development effect and sensitivity of the CDs, pixel profiles were generated for selected regions (indicated by the white strip) of FPs developed on aluminum foil, using both traditional powders and the optimized CDs (Fig. 8c). The ridge and furrow areas of the fingerprint remain distinctly visible, confirming that LFPs developed with CDs have significant potential for LFPs detection compared to traditional powders.

In practical crime investigations, LFPs are often found after some time has passed. As such, the LFPs that one comes across in real life are usually old rather than newly deposited. Fig. 9a illustrates the visualization of LFPs on glass slides after 1, 10, 20 and 60 days using the CDs in order to assess the visualization capacity for aged LFPs with physical degradation over time. The effect of temperature on the storage of LFPs on a marble surface is investigated using CDs. The FPs were stored at various temperatures (100–175 °C) for 10 min, as shown in Fig. 9b. When FPs were treated up to 150 °C, the FL intensity remained unchanged. However, at 175 °C, the FL intensity decreased. Fig. 9c illustrates the effect of aging on the CDs, specifically after exposure to 365 nm UV light with an intensity of 36 mW/cm² for 0, 60, 120 and 180 min. The few observable variations in LFP images, as depicted in the figure, demonstrate the CDs outstanding luminous stability.

The pixel profile of aged FPs is displayed in Fig. 9d. Longer aging durations cause the fluorescent signals in the LFP photographs to gradually decline. Nonetheless, across the exposure time range of 1–20 days, the aged LFPs at Levels II and III finely resolved ridge details are still clearly visible. After 60 days, several of the ridge features of the aged LFPs at Level II have diminished because to the loss of skin and secretions. For FPs that are 60 days old, the fine details of the sweat pores have been masked by the diffusion of leftover grease, causing the ridge details at Level III to vanish. Remarkably, certain ridge details of the aged LFPs at Level II are still detectable and useful for determining personal traits even after 20 days of aging. These findings imply that the CDs have a great deal of potential for use as a phosphor to visualize LFPs. In addition, pixel profiles of thermally treated FPs are displayed in Fig. 9d. As can be seen from the profiles, there is clear difference between ridges and furrow even after treating up to 175 °C. A quantitative analysis of the changes in LFPs under 365 nm UV radiations with an intensity of 36 mW/cm² is presented in Fig. 9d, which is based on the red value. Over the course of 180 min of UV exposure, the red value exhibits very minor variations, retaining a notable contrast between ridges and grooves. This suggests that CDs can preserve the clarity of LFP images even after extended exposure to UV light. Forensic LFP identification is probably more successful with CDs because of their resistance to aging under UV light.

Following the addition of CDs to the PVA, the composite film is initially transparent but eventually takes on a light brownish colour. Fig. 10a displays photos taken under both UV (365 nm) and daytime conditions. When exposed to UV light at 365 nm, the 1–5 wt% CDs@PVA NC films showed intense blue light emission. Moreover, PL experiments were carried out at 375 nm using the 1–5 wt% CDs@PVA NC films as an excitation source. Figure S5 illustrates how the PL intensity of the NC films rises as the CDs content does. One explanation could be that PVA many hydroxyl groups efficiently create hydrogen bonds, strengthening the carbon-oxygen bonds in the CDs and lowering non-radiative relaxation [93]. Good transparency can be shown in the optical images of CDs@PVA NC films with various CDs ratios (Fig. 10b). To satisfy the specifications for applications such organic light-emitting diodes, AC devices and security labels, luminescent polymeric materials need to have good flexibility in addition to strong mechanical strength [94]. The CDs@PVA NC films have a high degree of mechanical flexibility and deformation resistance. Their ability to sustain intense blue emission throughout the NC allowed them to be bent, twisted and folded into a variety of shapes, such as round, cone, cylindrical, spiral and boat forms (Fig. 10c and d).

For the practical use of promising materials, optical stability is an essential component. As a result, Figure S6a-c depicts the FL stability of the CDs@PVA NC film at aging, UV treatment and mechanical deformation. The effect of aging on the 5 wt% CDs@PVA NC film is evaluated over a period of up to 150 days, as shown in Figure S6a. The results indicate that the PL intensity remains at approximately 95 % (Figure S7a and b) even after 150 days of storage. Additionally, the photostability of the composite film is assessed by exposing it to intense UV illumination (36 mW/cm²) for various durations (0, 60, 90 and 180 min) (Figure S6b). After 180 min of continuous UV exposure, the CDs@PVA NC film retained 90 % of its PL intensity (Figure S7c and d). Additionally, to examine the mechanical deformation of the NC films, various loads (ranging from 0 to 7 tons for 10 min each) were applied and the resulting photographs under UV light are shown in Figure S6c. The results demonstrate that, even under a maximum load of 7 tons, 82 % of the PL intensity is retained (Figure S7e and f). The results indicate that the optimized film exhibits outstanding photostability, mechanical properties and consistent FL behavior, even under various deformation conditions. These attributes are essential for applications in wearable fluorescent devices [95,96].

A WCA analyzer is used to evaluate the PVA-based films water barrier properties. Among the media employed to measure the WCAs of PVA and 1–5 wt% CDs@PVA NC films were water, milk, juice and castor oil [97]. The results are illustrated in Figure S8a-d. The PVA film became more hydrophobic after the addition of CDs. With the 5 wt% CDs@PVA NC film, the contact angle improved to 102° for castor oil, 99° for juice, 98° for milk and 95° for water (Figure S8a-d). The dense structure that forms between PVA and CDs as a result of intra- and intermolecular hydrogen bonding is responsible for the NC films increased contact angle. El-Shamy [98] revealed a comparable outcome for CDs derived from glucose that were integrated into PVA films. As a result, the PVA-based films surface energy is further altered, decreasing their propensity to interact with polar solvents. As a result, water molecules create droplets that have less surface contact with the film, which raises the water contact angle. The 5 wt% CDs@PVA NC film had the largest contact angle of any tested media, measuring 102°, as demonstrated by the overall results. These results show that, out of all the films, the optimized NC film has the best hydrophobicity [99]. Figure S8e shows the variation of contact angle of PVA and 1–5 wt% CDs@PVA NC films on water, milk, juice and castor oil. As can be seen from the graphs, neat PVA films generally have relatively low contact angles because PVA is hydrophilic due to its hydroxyl groups. This means water tends to spread out on its surface, indicating good wettability. By modifying the contact angle, CDs can be used to tailor the surface properties of PVA films for specific applications, such as in packaging materials, biomedical applications, or sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multiple stimulus responsiveness shapes memory function
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen bonds are known to function as molecular switches that are highly tunable to variations in temperature, solvent composition and pH, among other parameters [100]. In order to evaluate the shape recovery and FL performance of the 5 wt% CDs@PVA NCs films, their structural capabilities were demonstrated in this field by cutting the films into thin strips. A traditional bending test is used to assess the shape memory behaviour and FL characteristics of the CDs@PVA NCs, taking into account a variety of stimuli like heat, pH and water. First, for 5 min, the straight strips (original forms) were baked at 80°C, which is greater than the glass transition temperature (Tg) of PVA, which can be found from the DSC curve in Figure S10a. After applying external force to fix their temporary shapes at about 30°, they were allowed to sit at room temperature for 5 min. The distorted strips were then submerged in various pH solutions (∼2, 7 and 13) to evaluate how well they recovered their shape. A camera is used to record every form change, as seen in Fig. 11a–d.

As seen in Fig. 11a, the deformed 5 wt% CDs@PVA strips gradually swelled and disintegrated after being soaked in deionized water (pH ∼7) at room temperature (25 °C) for about 110 sec. This caused the strips to become limp. With an increase in reaction time in the neutral aqueous solution, the samples FL steadily decreased. The FL of NCs did not, however, entirely vanish. The distorted CDs/PVA strips are shown in Fig. 11b and c, respectively, which show the impacts of alkaline and acidic environments. For distorted strips subjected to alkaline conditions, the optimal recovery of shape occurred at 170° in 140 sec and the NCs FL intensity raise as the immersion duration in the alkaline solution extended. The distorted strip recovered to a recovery angle of 175° under acidic stimulation in 70 sec, although its FL intensity decreased steadily as it recovered [101]. The FL of the strip gradually diminished after reverting to its previous straight shape. Furthermore, within 12 sec of being quickly submerged in a hot environment (75°C, Fig. 11d), the distorted CDs@PVA strips recovered an angle of 177°.

CDs@PVA NCs exhibit pH-responsive FL properties that are caused by the different ionization of carboxyl groups on their surface in acidic versus alkaline conditions. FL properties are altered as a result of this ionization, which modifies the groups between protonated and deprotonated forms. CDs carboxyl groups undergo protonation when exposed to acidic environments [102,103]. Electrons are easily absorbed by protonated carboxyl groups, which lowers the electron density on the surface of CDs and introduces defects. As a result, there is a lower chance of electron excitation and transitions, which lowers the FL intensity. The carboxyl groups on CDs surface deprotonate in an alkaline environment. More negative charges are present in deprotonated carboxyl groups than in their protonated counterparts. On the other hand, deprotonation lessens surface imperfections and improves the conjugation of the entire CDs system by reducing the capacity of carboxyl groups to absorb electrons from carbon nuclei. Because of this, the CDs@PVA NCs exhibit more FL intensity in an alkaline environment than in an acidic one. Hydrogen bond formation and dissolution between CDs and PVA can also be influenced by the protonation and deprotonation of carboxyl groups on the CD surface. As a result, in alkaline as opposed to acidic environments, the shape recovery rate is reduced. Thus, the applications of the CDs@PVA NCs can be tailored to certain settings by efficiently utilizing its pH and temperature-dependent variations in shape recovery rate and FL intensity. Certain applications can successfully take use of the CDs@PVA NCs capacity to modify its shape recovery rate and FL intensity in response to pH and temperature variations [104].

Fig. 11e and f provides additional research into the shape recovery capabilities of the CDs@PVA composite. A sample in the shape of a quatrefoil is made and folded to distort it. During the water-induced healing phase, it took around 120 sec in water and 80 sec in an EDTA solution for all the petals of the closed flower shape to fully open. This behaviour indicates that the CDs-containing PVA film demonstrated excellent shape memory properties in response to water and EDTA. Crucially, a commercial UV lamp (365 nm excitation) is able to watch the whole recovery process because to the CDs brilliant blue FL. A standard bending-recovery test under varied pH settings is used to quantitatively assess the water-induced shape memory impact of CDs@PVA in order to further validate the involvement of CDs in the shape recovery performance. With a recovery ratio higher than 97 %, all three composite specimens demonstrated exceptional water-induced shape memory capabilities, as seen in Fig. 11 g. Nonetheless, the water pH had a big impact on the response rate. It is clear that when submerged in water with a pH of ∼2, the original straight sample recovered in 70 sec. It took 110 sec for the CDs@PVA composite to fully recover when the water pH is raised to 7. After being submerged in room temperature water for around 140 sec, the distorted strip gradually regained its previous shape with a pH increase to 13. In contrast, the pure PVA film faced significant challenges in shape recovery after deformation, achieving only a 40 % recovery ratio even after 150 sec of immersion in water with a pH of approximately 7 (Fig. 11h). For water with pH values of ∼ 2 and ∼13, the recovery ratios were 42 % and 39 %, respectively. According to the current findings, when shape memory polymers (SMPs) are utilized in various situations, adding CDs to the PVA matrix not only improves the shape memory capabilities but also performs very well in terms of viewing the shape recovery process [105].

A simple experimental setup for determining the NCs films ability to block UV light is depicted in Fig. 12a. A UV digital lux meter is placed three centimetres beneath the polymer film holder [106]. A UV lamp or ambient light could be the light source for this experiment. The UV light intensity that penetrated the polymer film is measured using a digital UV lux meter. The intensity is measured for polymer films with varying concentrations of CDs under different light sources and the results are shown in Fig. 12a. The UV shielding mechanism of CDs within the PVA matrix is illustrated in Fig. 12b. A pure PVA polymer film without CDs provides shielding of 9.90 % for HEBL, 14.89 % for UV-A, 12.94 % for UV-B and 19.07 % for UV-C, respectively. The 1 wt% CDs@PVA film demonstrated blocking efficiencies of 15 % for HEBL, 53 % for UV-A, 39 % for UV-B and 66 % for UV-C (Fig. 12 c). Additionally, the UV shielding percentages for 3 % and 5 % CDs@PVA NC films are detailed in Table 3. The results show that the highest blocking percentages achieved were 61 % for HEBL, 80 % for UV-A, 75 % for UV-B and 85 % for UV-C. Therefore, 5 wt% CDs@PVA appears to be the optimal concentration for enhanced UV-C shielding performance [107].

A 61 % shielding of HEBL light is insufficient for effective UV protection. To enhance this, various thicker polymer films were prepared using a 5 wt% CDs-dispersed PVA solution. Fig. 12 d illustrates the UV shielding effectiveness of polymer films with varying thicknesses under HEBL, UV-A, UV-B and UV-C sources. The UV light intensity from direct daylight is measured at 3196 μW/cm². The 100 μm thick polymer film with 5 wt% CDs absorbs approximately 61 % of HEBL, 80 % of UV-A, 75 % of UV-B and 85 % of UV-C (Fig. 12d). In contrast, a similar thickness PVA polymer film absorbs only 10 % of HEBL, 11 % of UV-A, 13 % of UV-B and 18 % of UV-C. The maximum UV shielding recorded for a 250 μm thick PVA polymer film is 22 % for HEBL, 26 % for UV-A, 24 % for UV-B and 30 % for UV-C (Fig. 12e). This result highlights the significance of the UV shielding properties of CDs. As shown in Fig. 12d, the UV light absorption increases with the thickness of the polymer films. Among various thicknesses of NC films, the 250 μm thick 5 wt% CDs@PVA film demonstrated the highest UV shielding capabilities, with 79 % for HEBL, 93 % for UV-A, 88 % for UV-B and 98 % for UV-C. These results show a significant enhancement in UV shielding performance with increased film thickness, regardless of the light source. This observation demonstrates that optimizing the polymer films thickness is essential for improving UV shielding efficacy. While ZnO nanoparticles are said to be able to block over 90 % of UV light in certain papers, these claims are usually based on qualitative analysis of UV–visible spectral data. In contrast, our study provides a quantitative analysis of UV shielding capability using a UV digital lux meter. The high level of light scattering and reflection offered by the CDs, which effectively convert UV light photon energy into heat, is responsible for the polymer films improved UV shielding efficacy [108]. Furthermore, a comparison is made between the transparency of the 250 μm thick CDs@PVA NC film and that of pure PVA polymer. Photographic pictures of the PVA and 1–5 wt% CDs@PVA NC films are shown in Fig. 10a. The films transparency is essentially unaltered, with only a faint color tinge visible. Figure S9 illustrates the comparative graph that is made for pristine PVA, N-ZnO and 5 wt% CDs@PVA NC at the same 250 μm thickness in order to comprehend the function of CDs in the polymer film. The UV-A shielding capabilities of pristine PVA, N-ZnO and 5 wt% CDs@PVA NC films were calculated to be approximately 26 %, 60 % and 93 %, respectively. Because of the surface modification of CDs, which promotes their dispersion in the polymer matrix, UV light shielding between N-ZnO and 5 wt% CDs@PVA NC has improved by over thirty percent. This method preserves great transparency by improving the compatibility between the CDs and the polymer. As a result, a carefully considered quantity of CDs guarantees ideal dispersion in the polymer matrix, considerably increasing UV shielding capacity without going overboard. Consequently, it is determined that CDs-dispersed polymer films represent a viable substitute for shielding UV-sensitive components [109].

TGA and DSC thermograms (first heating cycle) demonstrate the thermal characteristics of CDs-embedded composite films (Figure S10a and b). The melting point (Tm) of plain PVA is approximately 228 °C, as shown in Figure S10a. The melting temperature rises slightly for the composite films. The 5 wt% CDs@PVA composite, for instance, exhibits a maximum shift and a melting temperature of 242 °C. Furthermore, when the concentration of CDs rises, the melting enthalpy falls. It is clear that when CD concentration rises, the degree of crystallinity falls. This implies that CDs serve as fillers for nanoparticles by introducing structural disorder within the polymer matrix. This disorder is probably caused by strong molecular interactions, including the hydrogen bonding between PVA and CDs, which lowers the degree of crystallinity [110]. According to the TGA data (Figure S10b), water removal occurs between 50 °C and 150 °C and the remaining water content in the samples ranges from roughly 5–15 %. Peak thermal decomposition occurs between 255 and 375 °C and the addition of CDs to the PVA matrix modifies the host polymers decomposition behaviour somewhat. For the 5 wt% CDs composite, the temperature at which the greatest weight loss takes place rises from 375 °C for pure PVA to 450 °C. Pure PVA breaks down completely before it reaches 600 °C, whereas NCs containing more CDs leave about 18 % of carbonized residues behind [111].

The typical stress-strain responses for PVA and 1–5 wt% CDs@PVA NCs are displayed in Figure S11. Table 4 provides an overview of the mechanical parameters that were collected, such as toughness (T), elongation at break (εb), ultimate tensile strength (σs) and Youngs modulus (Y). The CDs@PVA NC has the following properties: εb of 29.94 ± 43 %, T of 2924 ± 25 J/m³, σs of 107.35 ± 45 MPa and Y of 373.5 ± 10 MPa. These values imply that the tensile properties of the polymeric matrix are modified by the creation of bonds between the chains of the blend components, improving the network structure in comparison to pure PVA polymers [112]. The tensile properties of the CDs@PVA NCs films have significantly increased, particularly in terms of T, σs and Y. This improvement can be credited to the inclusion of CDs to the polymer blend, which formed a second bonded network. Strong electrostatic contacts between PVA and CDs as well as hydrogen bonding allow this network to be formed. When 5 wt% CDs are added, the values of Y and σs rise from 152.5 ± 25–373.5 ± 10 MPa and 63.71 ± 52–107.35 ± 45 MPa. These improvements show the mechanical robustness of CDs@PVA films. Furthermore, these filled films show increased toughness; the CDs@PVA film reaches a maximum value of 2924 ± 25 J/m³. The filling technique has minimal effect on the blends εb, which drops from 34.26 ± 53 % for PVA to 29.94 ± 43 % for 5 wt% CDs@PVA films. Together with the produced NCs significant rise in T, this slight decrease in εb suggests that these films maintain their mechanical flexibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Utilization of CDs for preservation of tomatoes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 13a illustrates the schematic representation of the tomato preservation process using the dip coating method [113]. In this study, tomatoes were stored at room temperature (24 °C) and evaluated daily. The tomatoes were individually dipped into PVA, 5 wt% CDs and 5 wt% CDs@PVA solutions, with one group left untreated as the control (Fig. 13b-e). To evaluate oxidation levels, we inspected the tomatoes for the presence of black regions or spots on their skins. It is observed that the control group developed black spots and deteriorated within 16 days of storage (Fig. 13b). In contrast, the tomatoes coated with 5 wt% CDs@PVA remained fresh (Fig. 13e). Tomatoes dipped in PVA and CDs showed fewer black spots compared to the control group (Fig. 13c and d). After 36 days of storage, the uncoated tomatoes had almost entirely deteriorated and emitted an unpleasant odor due to the breakdown of sugars and other compounds as they became overripe [114]. In contrast, tomatoes coated with 5 wt% CDs@PVA remained in better condition, while those coated with PVA and CDs showed similar levels of damage. Over the 36-day period, the tomatoes experienced size reduction and weight loss due to water loss. However, tomatoes coated with 5 wt% CDs@PVA showed minimal fungal growth, likely due to the presence of CDs, which enhanced water solubility and permeability (Fig. 13e).

The cytotoxicity of the synthesized CDs is assessed using the MTT assay method with SH-SY-5Y (Neuro-Blastoma) cells (Figure S12a and b). The SH-SY-5Y cells were cultivated at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 in fresh Dulbeccos Modified Eagles Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10 % Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS). In 96-well plates, they were planted with 5000 cells per well and they were incubated for 24 h. The viability and compatibility of the CDs were assessed by preparing them at different concentrations (50, 75, 100, 125 and 150 μL) from a stock solution (0.25 mg/mL in phosphate-buffered saline). Following incubation, the CDs showed only modest toxicity to SH-SY-5Y cells, with cell viability varying from 43.8 % to 86.6 % based on the concentration of CDs utilized (Figure S12c). The luminous CDs appear to be suited for bio-imaging applications based on their reduced cytotoxicity [115].

In this study, we explored the potential of using CDs as invisible fluorescent ink. An aqueous solution of CDs is applied using both brush and screen-printing techniques [116]. Subsequently, various patterns and designs were created on glass, glossy sheets, plastic and Whatman paper. Additionally, screen-printed AC patterns were created on bond paper, marble, aluminum foil and glossy sheets. Digital images of these patterns, captured under daylight and UV 365 nm light, are displayed in Fig. 14 a & b. Under direct light, the letters appeared colourless and the FL vanished immediately when the UV lamp is turned off, demonstrating the necessary hidden feature for secret AC marks [117–119]. However, under UV 365 nm irradiation, the letters exhibited bright blue FL. Additionally, the fluorescent ink produced is clear, durable, non-toxic and easily removable. This makes the synthesized CDs suitable for use in fluorescent pens or AC applications. But when the UV lamp is switched off, the patterns were no longer easily noticeable in the daylight. After soaking in the CDs solution for 2 h, the handmade paper slices showed brilliant FL under 385 nm illumination (Fig. 14c and d). In contrast, under 365 nm excitation light, paper slices that had not been soaked in the CDs solution exhibited no FL emission. When combined, these characteristics allow for the encryption of highly secure information, such as unique banknote logos [120,121].

To assess the durability of CDs ink, the fluorescent intensity of selected patterns screen-printed on bond paper, glossy sheet, marble and aluminum foil is evaluated after soaking, rubbing and crumpling. The AC pattern on bond paper is used to compare changes in color. The excellent wet fastness of the ink is demonstrated in Fig. 15a and b. The pattern showed minimal damage after being soaked in water for 3 days and treated with acid for 120 min. Even after 3 days in water and 120 min of acid treatment, the fluorescent colours luminosity dropped by only 3.5 units (Figure S13a and b), a result attributed to the hydrophobic groups in the CDs. The inks resistance to friction is relatively low. When the AC patterns on the marble substrate were rubbed with emery sandpaper, there is minimal impact on the FL. However, noticeable defects appeared after 20 and 40 cycles of rubbing as shown in Fig. 15c. Despite a significant decrease in maximum FL (Figure S13c), the underlying ink remained intact due to its excellent adhesion fastness, thereby preserving its AC functionality. Fig. 15d shows that the AC pattern on the al. foil remained visible under 365 nm irradiation even after being curled, folded and violently crumpled for 2, 6 and 10 min. Although the fluorescent effect varied due to reduced surface flatness, the change in luminosity is less than 10 % (Figure S13d). CDs enhance the inks flexibility, allowing it to endure severe deformation. Overall, the exceptional dry and wet fastness indicates that CDs provide PVA ink with broad practical applicability.

In summary, CDs derived from ESM were synthesized using a simple microwave method in 5 min. The resultant CDs were characterized physico-chemically and revealed to have a quasi-spherical lamellar structure with an interlayer spacing of 0.22 nm and a mean diameter of 4.3 nm. Additionally, the CDs serve as a useful nanoprobe for the quick dual-mode detection of EDTA and Fe3+. Fe3+ considerably quenches the surface state emission through a combination of static and dynamic quenching methods. Aggregation from the complexation of CDs and Fe3+ leads to the static quenching effect. The decrease in average lifespan indicates that dynamic quenching takes place as a result of electrons moving from CDs to the unoccupied d orbitals of Fe3+. By adding EDTA, which engages in a redox process with Fe3+, the quenched emission of CDs can be re-established. Furthermore, the FL outcomes made it possible to build a molecular logic gate with many inputs, opening the door for the creation of FL-based smart molecular devices. The CDs help the PVA matrix generate hydrogen bond-based physical crosslinking networks, which is why the CDs@PVA composite outperforms pure PVA in terms of shape memory effects caused by water and EDTA. Additionally, the shape recovery ratio of the CDs-containing NCs increased when the pH of the water increased, suggesting that the NCs response to EDTA and water can be adjusted. Additionally, the CDs provided blue FL under 365 nm excitation, which is utilized for fluorescent imaging of LFPs on various forensically significant substrates. The LFPs images developed exhibited high contrast and excellent resolution with minimal background interference. By lowering fungal growth, spoiling and moisture loss at room temperature, the CDs@PVA covering extended shelf life. The results suggest that fluorescent CDs have potential applications as antioxidants, antimicrobials and anti-adhesive additives. They might be applied to the creation of UV-blocking coatings and perishable food packaging, with possible applications in the pharmaceutical and health sectors. Interestingly, the prepared CDs demonstrated low cytotoxicity and high selectivity for Fe3+, making them suitable for detecting Fe3+ in SH-SY-5Y cells. Additionally, the CDs photoluminescence properties, characterized by a broad excitation range and stable FL, enhance their effectiveness in AC applications. This research offers a viable approach for expanding the use of bio-based inks in the printing industry.
